audioscript.text: Part 1, you will hear a woman from a job agency giving information to a man about work in a chain of restaurants.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, I understand you're interested in restaurant work.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I've got a bit of experience, and I can provide references.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's good, I can check all that later.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Now, Milo's restaurants have some vacancies at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: They're a really good company to work for, lots of benefits.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: They've got a very good reputation for looking after staff.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: For example, all employees get training, even temporary staff.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, really?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's quite unusual, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Certainly is.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And do staff get free uniforms too?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Um. You just need to wear a white T-shirt and black trousers, it says here, so I guess not.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: But another benefit of working for a big company like this is that you can get a discount at any of their restaurants.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Even at weekends?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: No, but you'll be working then anyway.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh yes, I suppose so.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Um. Most of their restaurants are in the city center, aren't they?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So easy to get to by bus.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: But if you have to do a late shift and finish work after midnight,
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: the company will pay for you to get a taxi home.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I probably won't need one.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I think I'd use my bike.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Now, they do have some quite specific requirements for the kind of person they're looking for.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Milo's is a young dynamic company, and they're really keen on creating a strong team.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's really important that you can fit in and get on well with everyone.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yep, I've got no problem with that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It sounds good actually.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The last place I worked for was quite demanding too, we had to make sure we gave a really high level of service.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's good to hear, because that will be equally important at Milo's.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I know they want people who have an eye for detail.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm very used to working in that kind of environment.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Perfect.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So the only other thing that's required is good communication skills.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So you'll need to have a certificate in English.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK. Let's have a look at the current job vacancies at Milo's.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The first one is in Wivenhoe street.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sorry, where?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Wivenhoe.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: W I V E N H O E.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's quite central, just off Cork street.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: They're looking for a breakfast supervisor.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That would be OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So you're probably familiar with the kind of responsibilities involved, obviously checking that all the portions are correct, et cetera.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And then things like checking all the procedures for cleaning the equipment are being followed.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, and what about the salary?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: In my last job, I was getting £9.50 per hour.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I was hoping to get a bit more than that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, to begin with, you'd be getting £9.75, but that goes up to £11.25 after three months.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's not too bad.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And I suppose it's a very early start.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Mmm, that's the only unattractive thing about this job. But then you have the afternoons and evenings free, so the restaurant starts serving breakfast from 7:00 am.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And you'd have to be there at 5:30 to set everything up, but you'd be finished at 12:30.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Mmm. Well, as you say, there are advantages to that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Now you might also be interested in the job at the City Road branch.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's for a junior chef, so again a position of responsibility.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I might prefer that, actually.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right, well obviously this role would involve supporting the sous chef and other senior staff, and you'd be responsible for making sure there's enough stock each week.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And sorting out all the deliveries.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I've never done that before, but I imagine it's fairly straightforward once you get the hang of it.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, and you'd be working alongside more experienced staff to begin with.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So I'm sure it wouldn't be a problem.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The salary is slightly higher here. It's an annual salary of 23,000 pounds.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I know that if they like you it's likely you'll be promoted quite quickly, so that's worth thinking about.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it does sound interesting.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What are the hours like?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The usual, I think.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: There's a lot of evening and weekend work, but they're closed on Mondays.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: But you do get one Sunday off every 4 weeks, so would you like me to send off your...
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear a talk to members of the public about plans for a new housing development near a town called Nunston.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello everyone, it's good to see that so many members of the public have shown up for our presentation on the new housing development planned on the outskirts of Nunston.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: I'm Mark Reynolds, and I'm Communications Manager at the development.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: I'll start by giving you a brief overview of our plans for the development.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: So, one thing I'm sure you'll want to know is why we've selected this particular site for a housing development.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: At present, it's being used for farming.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: Like much of the land around Nunston, but because of the new industrial center in Nunston, there's a lot of demand for housing for employees in the region.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: As many employees are having to commute long distances at present.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: Of course, there's also the fact that we have an international airport.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: Just 20 minutes drive away, but although that's certainly convenient, it wasn't one of our major criteria for choosing the site.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: We were more interested in the fact that there's an excellent hospital, just 15 km away, and a large secondary school, even closer than that.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: One drawback to the site is that it's on quite a steep slope, but we've taken account of that in our planning.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: So it shouldn't be a major problem.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: We've had a lot of positive feedback about the plans.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: People like the wide variety of accommodation types and prices.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: And the fact that it's only a short drive to get out into the countryside from the development.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: We were particularly pleased that so many people like the designs for the layout of the development.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: With the majority of people saying it generally made a good impression, and blended in well with the natural features of the landscape.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: With provision made for protecting trees and wildlife on the site.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: Some people have mentioned that they'd like to see more facilities for cyclists, and we'll look at that, but the overall feedback has been that the design and facilities of the development make it seem a place where people of all ages can live together happily.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, so I'll put a map of the proposed development up on the screen.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: You'll see it's bounded on the south side by the main road, which then goes on to Nunston.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: Another boundary is formed by London Road.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: On the western side of the development, inside the development, there'll be about 400 houses and three apartment blocks.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: There'll also be a school for children up to 11 years old.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: If you look at the south entrance at the bottom of the map, there's a road from there that goes right up through the development.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: The school will be on that road, at the corner of the second turning to the left.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: A large sports center is planned with facilities for indoor and outdoor activities.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: This will be on the western side of the development, just below the road that branches off from London Road.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: There'll be a clinic where residents can go if they have any health problems.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: Can you see the lake towards the top of the map?
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: The clinic will be just below this, to the right of a street of houses.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: There'll also be a community center for people of all ages.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: On the northeast side of the development, there'll be a row of specially designed houses, specifically for residents over 65 and the community center will be adjoining this.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: We haven't forgotten about shopping.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: There'll be a supermarket between the two entrances to the development.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: We're planning to leave the three large trees near London Road, and it'll be just to the south of these.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: It's planned to have a playground for younger children.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: If you look at the road that goes up from the south entrance, you'll see it curves round to the left at the top.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: And the playground will be in that curve with nice views of the lake.
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: OK, so now does anyone have any quest...
audioscript.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: On the western side of the development, inside the development, there'll be about 400 houses and three apartment blocks.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: There'll also be a school for children up to 11 years old.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: If you look at the south entrance at the bottom of the map, there's a road from there that goes right up through the development.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: The school will be on that road, at the corner of the second turning to the left.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: A large sports center is planned with facilities for indoor and outdoor activities.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: This will be on the western side of the development, just below the road that branches off from London Road.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: There'll be a clinic where residents can go if they have any health problems.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: Can you see the lake towards the top of the map?
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: The clinic will be just below this, to the right of a street of houses.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: There'll also be a community center for people of all ages.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: On the northeast side of the development, there'll be a row of specially designed houses, specifically for residents over 65 and the community center will be adjoining this.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: We haven't forgotten about shopping.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: There'll be a supermarket between the two entrances to the development.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: We're planning to leave the three large trees near London Road, and it'll be just to the south of these.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: It's planned to have a playground for younger children.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: If you look at the road that goes up from the south entrance, you'll see it curves round to the left at the top.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: And the playground will be in that curve with nice views of the lake.
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: OK, so now does anyone have any quest...
utterances.speaker: MARK REYNOLDS

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: What are the TWO main reasons why this site has been chosen for the housing development?
options: It has suitable geographical features.
There is easy access to local facilities.
It has good connections with the airport.
The land is of little agricultural value.
It will be convenient for workers.

body.title: Which TWO aspects of the planned housing development have people given positive feedback about?
options: the facilities for cyclists
the impact on the environment
the encouragement of good relations between residents
the low cost of all the accommodation
the rural location

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two geography students, Adam and Michelle, talking about a volcanic eruption which took place in Iceland in 1783.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, Michelle, shall we make a start on our presentation?
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: We haven't got that much time left.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: No, Adam, but at least we've done all the background reading.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: I found it really interesting.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: I'd never even heard of the Laki eruption before this.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Me neither.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: I suppose 1783 is a long time ago.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: But it was a huge eruption, and it had such devastating consequences.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: I know.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: It was great there were so many primary sources to look at.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: It really gives you a sense of how catastrophic the volcano was.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: People were really trying to make sense of the science for the first time.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: But what I found more significant was how it impacted directly and indirectly on political events, as well as having massive social and economic consequences.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: I know.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: That should be the main focus of our presentation.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: The observations made by people at the time were interesting, weren't they?
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: I mean, they all gave a pretty consistent account of what happened.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Even if they didn't always use the same terminology.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Yeah, I was surprised there were so many weather stations established by that time.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: So you know, you can see how the weather changed often by the hour.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Writers at the time talked about the Laki haze to describe the volcanic fog that spread across Europe.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: They all realized that this wasn't the sort of fog they were used to, and of course, this was in pre-industrial times, so they hadn't experienced sulfur smelling fog before.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: No, that's true.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Reports from the period blamed the haze for an increase in headaches, respiratory issues, and asthma attacks.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: And they all describe how it covered the sun.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: And made it look a strange red color.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Hmm, must have been very weird.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Ha, it's interesting that Benjamin Franklin wrote about the haze.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Did you read that?
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: He was the American ambassador in Paris at the time.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Yeah, at first no one realized that the haze was caused by the volcanic eruption in Iceland.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: It was Benjamin Franklin who realized that before anyone else.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: He's often credited with that apparently, but a French naturalist beat him to it.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: I can't remember his name, I'd have to look it up.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Then other naturalists had the same idea.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: All independently of each other.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: We should talk about the immediate impact of the eruption, which was obviously enormous.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Especially in Iceland where so many people died.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Hmm, you'd expect that, and the fact that the volcanic ash drifted so swiftly, but not that the effects would go on for so long.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Or that two years after the eruption, strange weather events were being reported as far away as North America and North Africa.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: No, I found all that hard to believe too.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: It must have been terrible, and there was nothing anyone could do about it, even if they knew the ash cloud was coming in their direction.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: We should run through some of the terrible consequences of the eruption experienced in different countries.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: There's quite a varied range.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Starting with Iceland,
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: where the impact on farming was devastating.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Hmm, one of the most dramatic things there was the effect on livestock as they grazed in the fields.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: They were poisoned because they ate vegetation that had been contaminated with fluorine as a result of the volcanic fallout.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: That was horrible. In Egypt.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: The bizarre weather patterns led to a severe drought, and as a result the Nile didn't flood, which meant the crops all failed.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: It's so far from where the eruption happened.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: And yet the famine there led to more people dying than any other country.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: It was worse than the plague.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: OK, then in the UK the mortality rate went up a lot.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Presumably from respiratory illnesses, according to one report, it was about double the usual number, and included an unusually high percentage of people under the age of 25.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Hmm. I think people will be surprised to hear that the weather in the USA was badly affected too.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: George Washington even makes a note in his diary.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: That they were snowbound until March in Virginia.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: That was before he became president.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Yes, and there was ice floating down the Mississippi, which was unprecedented.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Huh. Astonishing, really.
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: Anyway, what do you think we should include next?
audioscript.speaker: MICHELLE

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Why do the students think the Laki eruption of 1783 is so important?
options: It was the most severe eruption in modern times.
It led to the formal study of volcanoes.
It had a profound effect on society.

items.title: What surprised Adam about observations made at the time?
options: the number of places producing them
the contradictions in them
the lack of scientific data to support them

items.title: According to Michelle, what did the contemporary sources say about the Laki haze?
options: People thought it was similar to ordinary fog.
It was associated with health issues.
It completely blocked out the sun for weeks.

items.title: Adam corrects Michelle when she claims that Benjamin Franklin
options: came to the wrong conclusion about the cause of the haze.
was the first to identify the reason for the haze.
supported the opinions of other observers about the haze.

body.title: Which TWO issues following the Laki eruption surprised the students?
options: how widespread the effects were
how long-lasting the effects were
the number of deaths it caused
the speed at which the volcanic ash cloud spread
how people ignored the warning signs

text: What comment do the students make about the impact of the Laki eruption on the following countries?
textReadable: What comment do the students make about the impact of the Laki eruption on the following countries?

list: This country suffered the most severe loss of life
The impact on agriculture was predictable.
There was a significant increase in deaths of young people.
This country saw the highest rise in food prices in the world.
It caused a particularly harsh winter.
body.title: Countries

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear a student on a fashion design course giving a talk about the history of pockets.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now we've been asked to choose an aspect of European clothing or fashion, and to talk about its development over time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I decided to focus on a rather small area of clothing, and that's pockets.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I chose pockets for two reasons really.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We all have them in jeans.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Jackets, coats for example, and even though we often carry bags or briefcases as well, nothing is quite as convenient as being able to pop your phone or credit card into your pocket.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Yet, I suspect that other than that, people don't really think about pockets too much, and they're rather overlooked as a fashion item.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's certainly very interesting to go back in time, and see how pockets developed for men and women.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the 18th century, fashions were quite different from the way they are now.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Pockets were too.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If we think about male fashion first, that was the time when suits became popular.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Trousers were knee length only, and referred to as breeches.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The waistcoats were short, and the jackets were long, but all three garments were lined with material, and pockets were sewn into this cloth, by whichever tailor the customer used.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The wearer could then carry small objects such as pencils or coins on their person, and reach them through a gap in the lining.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Coat pockets became increasingly decorative on the outside for men who wanted to look stylish, but they were often larger, but plainer, if the wearer was someone with a profession who needed to carry medical instruments, a doctor or physician for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The development of women's pockets was a little different.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For one thing, they weren't nearly as visible or as easy to reach as men's.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the 18th and 19th centuries, women carried numerous possessions on their person, and some of these could be worth a lot of money.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Women were more vulnerable to theft, and wealthy women in particular worried constantly about pickpockets.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, what they did was to have a pair of pockets made.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That were tied together with string.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The pockets were made of fabric, which might be recycled cloth if the wearer had little money, or something more expensive.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Such as linen, sometimes featuring very delicate embroidery.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Women tied the pockets around their waist, so that they hung beneath their clothes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Remember, skirts were long then, and there was plenty of room to hide a whole range of small possessions between the layers of petticoats that were commonly worn.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They would have an opening in the folds of their skirts, through which they could reach whatever they needed, like their perfume.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Working women of course, also needed to carry around items that they might use for whatever job or trade they were involved in, but their pairs of pockets still remained on the inside of their clothing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They just got bigger or longer, sometimes reaching down to their knees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, the tie-on pockets went well into the 19th century, and only changed when fashion altered towards the end of that period.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's when dresses became tighter and less bulky, and the pairs of pockets became very noticeable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They stood out too much, and detracted from the woman's image.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Women who had been used to carrying around a range of personal possessions, and still wanted to, needed somewhere to carry these items about their person.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That was when small bags or pouches as they were known came into fashion, and of course, they inevitably led on to the handbag of more modern times.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Particularly when fashion removed pockets altogether.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: The wearer could then carry small objects such as pencils or coins on their person, and reach them through a gap in the lining.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Coat pockets became increasingly decorative on the outside for men who wanted to look stylish, but they were often larger, but plainer, if the wearer was someone with a profession who needed to carry medical instruments, a doctor or physician for example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The development of women's pockets was a little different.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For one thing, they weren't nearly as visible or as easy to reach as men's.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the 18th and 19th centuries, women carried numerous possessions on their person, and some of these could be worth a lot of money.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Women were more vulnerable to theft, and wealthy women in particular worried constantly about pickpockets.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, what they did was to have a pair of pockets made.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That were tied together with string.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The pockets were made of fabric, which might be recycled cloth if the wearer had little money, or something more expensive.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Such as linen, sometimes featuring very delicate embroidery.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Women tied the pockets around their waist, so that they hung beneath their clothes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Remember, skirts were long then, and there was plenty of room to hide a whole range of small possessions between the layers of petticoats that were commonly worn.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They would have an opening in the folds of their skirts, through which they could reach whatever they needed, like their perfume.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Working women of course, also needed to carry around items that they might use for whatever job or trade they were involved in, but their pairs of pockets still remained on the inside of their clothing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They just got bigger or longer, sometimes reaching down to their knees.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, the tie-on pockets went well into the 19th century, and only changed when fashion altered towards the end of that period.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's when dresses became tighter and less bulky, and the pairs of pockets became very noticeable.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They stood out too much, and detracted from the woman's image.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Women who had been used to carrying around a range of personal possessions, and still wanted to, needed somewhere to carry these items about their person.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That was when small bags or pouches as they were known came into fashion, and of course, they inevitably led on to the handbag of more modern times.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Particularly when fashion removed pockets altogether.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: They are <input> but can be overlooked by consumers and designers.
items.title: Reason for choice of subject
